input.headline: I do get jealous and question whether I’m cut out for non-monogamy

raw.headline: I do get jealous and question whether I’m cut out for non-monogamy

winner: I tried open relationships once. It was fine until my partner brought home a third wheel—turns out, that's just a bicycle without a kickstand.

candidates.text: I never thought I was cut out for monogamy—turns out my problem is just that I have a *mono*-gaming habit; I’ll play one game at a time, thanks.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Smart wordplay on 'mono' and 'non-monogamy,' concise and surprising with a clear connection to the headline."}

candidates.text: I'm not cut out for non-monogamy either—every time my partner leaves the room, I start rearranging all their furniture to make them feel less welcome when they return.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on jealousy with a specific, relatable scenario that's both funny and cleverly worded."}

candidates.text: I told my partner I was open to non-monogamy, and she said, "Just make sure you don’t get jealous—it’s not a personality type, it’s a commitment issue.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, specific, and delivers a surprising twist with a clever observation about jealousy and commitment issues in non-monogamy."}

candidates.text: I tried open relationships once. It was fine until my partner brought home a third wheel—turns out, that's just a bicycle without a kickstand.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke has a decent setup and a clever twist with the 'third wheel' pun, but the humor is somewhat generic and the surprise factor is limited. The specificity of the situation could be stronger."}

candidates.text: Why did the monogamous person bring a ladder to their relationship? Because they always wanted to *climb* back on!
